artifacts.content:
Subject: Drafting the truck agenda & all the baggage behind it

Em,

I stayed a little late at City Hall tonight to clean up the write‑up for the February 3, 2026 P&F Committee and main council agenda – the “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet” item I’ve been talking about. On paper it’s all neutral: Unit #23 is a 2006 International with 185,000 miles and 10,000 hours, 11 breakdowns over the last three winters, $18,500 in repairs in the last 24 months, recommended replacement per the 2025–2029 CIP, state contract purchase of a 2026 Freightliner 114SD from Nuss for $298,750 out of the Equipment Replacement Fund.

What I *didn’t* put in the memo is all the messy stuff that’s actually driving me. I keep thinking about that February 23–24, 2023 blizzard when Unit 23 died by Garfield and Mom slipped on the unplowed stretch of Oakridge at 6:30 a.m., broke her hip, and ended up at Essentia–St. Joe’s. She’s still in PT twice a week. Or Hannah’s January 2, 2025 slide‑off on County Road 3 by Harrison in her old 2010 Civic after the underbody scraper quit and the deputy basically told me the surface was slick because we didn’t get a good cut.

And then there was my own January 14, 2024 Essentia ER night after that 80‑hour week of storms and overtime, when Dr. Alicia Nguyen kept saying “this looks like stress, not a heart attack, but you can’t keep this pace.” I’m not going to write any of that into a public packet, but it’s all in my head when I argue that this isn’t just a shiny new toy.

Anyway, I’ll be home by 7. Save me some of whatever you and Jake are throwing together before practice.

– M

metadata.subject: Drafting the truck agenda & all the baggage behind it

artifacts.content:
Subject: Catch‑up on Mom, Dan, and this never‑ending plow saga

Dan,

Been meaning to write something longer than a text. Winter’s been a grind. Unit #23 has now officially crapped out during 11 snow operations over the last three seasons. We’ve dumped over $42,000 into drivetrain and hydraulic work in the last 18 months alone, on top of the $18,500 in big repairs the last two years. It’s the same feeling as watching you drown in costs on the Baxter trucking business before the Chapter 7 in March 2024 – eventually you stop pretending “one more fix” will turn it around.

I thought about that a lot during Mom’s oncology appointments at the St. Joseph’s–Essentia Cancer Center this winter. Since her Stage III breast cancer diagnosis in November 2024 I’ve burned through most of my vacation and comp time getting her to Brainerd for chemo and scans. Twice I’ve had to cancel because Unit 23 died and I had to jump in and plow the northeast sector myself. It’s infuriating.

I know you floated the idea last spring in the hallway at the Crow Wing County Courthouse – you and your bankruptcy attorney trying to structure some lease‑to‑own deal on a used plow through your old Baxter contacts. I appreciate the thought, but I’m glad we both agreed it would look terrible to have you financing City equipment while your Chapter 7 was still fresh ink.

On top of all that, I still think about your DWI/property damage mess when you clipped the City’s 2014 International on Laurel in January 2024. The restitution checks that started in October 2024 – the $12,600 – are literally showing up in the same spreadsheets I’m using to justify the new Freightliner. Talk about awkward.

Anyway, we’re taking Mom to PT Thursday, then probably Sunday dinner at her place if she’s up for it. You three should come up if the roads cooperate.

Mike

metadata.subject: Catch‑up on Mom, Dan, and this never‑ending plow saga

artifacts.content:
Title: Personal notes – why this truck matters more than the specs

Unit #23 facts (for my own head): 2006 International, 185k miles, 10k hours. Out of service during 11 snow events in the last three winters, including the February 26, 2025 storm when I was covering Mark Jensen’s night route because he’s on restricted duty after the alcohol treatment relapse at Essentia–Brainerd. Truck died again, I ended up plowing the northeast sector until 3:30 a.m. while Hannah was home alone still shaken from the panic attack at Brainerd High the week before.

Other nights burned into my brain:

• January 18, 2025 – breakdown near Forestview. Left the route to rush Mom from Oakridge to Essentia–Brainerd Clinic for an emergency cardiology appointment after her chest pain.

• February 11, 2025 – Unit 23 stalled by Brainerd High just as I was trying to leave for a 4:30 counseling session at Northern Pines. Missed it. Still feel the ripple from that in my sleep.

• December 9–10, 2024 – hydraulic failure by St. Joe’s; called Mark in for a 6‑hour overtime shift, blew the pay‑period OT line by $1,150, which is hanging over me after Karen Schultz’s October 2024 HR warning about overtime control.

• November 26, 2024 – electrical failure at Washington & 6th. Sat nearly three hours waiting on a tow while Thomas McNeil from 315 S 7th called my city email and then my personal cell repeatedly, swearing and threatening a complaint because his driveway apron stayed windrowed until after 11 p.m.

None of that will ever show up in a council packet, but it’s why I’m pushing so hard to get the 2026 Freightliner 114SD ordered now, through the state contract with Nuss, even if it means living with 10–12 month lead times.

metadata.title: Personal notes – why this truck matters more than the specs

artifacts.content:
Subject: Mom, Hannah, and me being a nervous wreck about storms

Sarah,

I owe you an update on everyone before you and Dan come up next weekend.

Mom: besides the cancer stuff you already know from Dan, she’s still dealing with the fallout from that February 24, 2023 fall on Oakridge during the blizzard when our plow broke down by Garfield Elementary. The ortho at Essentia–St. Joe’s keeps saying the hip fracture was made worse by how long the street sat unplowed. PT twice a week as of January 2025, and now we’re staring down her knee replacement on March 3, 2025 at Essentia. I’ve got intermittent FMLA lined up for March–April so I can split time between vendor visits for the new plow and helping her at the Maple Street house.

Kids: Hannah’s still jumpy in the car. That January 2, 2025 slide‑off on County Road 3 by Harrison in her Civic rattled her. The Crow Wing County deputy basically said if we’d had better cutting action with the underbody scraper that night, the road might not have been so slick. Add that to the January 9, 2024 near‑miss at Oakridge & Riverside when the old 2009 plow was down at the Thiesse Road garage for brake work and the intersection was a mess, and you can see why she hates winter driving.

Jacob’s plugging along with hockey and still babying that knee from the March 2023 ACL surgery at Cuyuna. Emily and I are still staring at about $18,700 in out‑of‑pocket bills from that.

Me: I’m trying to keep my own stress in check after that January 14, 2024 ER “episode” at Essentia. Northern Pines counseling helps when I can actually make it and the trucks cooperate.

We’ll have chili on Sunday if you all can make the drive.

Mike

metadata.subject: Mom, Hannah, and me being a nervous wreck about storms

artifacts.content:
Title: Council Mtg – Plow Truck Authorization (Unit #23 Replacement)

Time: Tuesday, February 3, 2026 – 6:00–7:30 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Council, Finance Director Karen Olson, City Engineer Karen Schultz, Michael Larson (presenter)

Notes:

• Agenda item: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet” – NOT any of my half‑joking personal titles like “after Hannah’s hit‑and‑run near Brainerd High” or “so I don’t end up back in Essentia ER.” Keep it dry.

• Key facts to hit: Unit #23 – 2006 International, 185,000+ miles, 10,000 hours, 11 snow events with breakdowns in last three winters (northeast sector delays, East River Road complaint from Sarah Thompson on Jan 8, 2025 re: her husband in a wheelchair nearly missing dialysis transport). Recent repair tally ~ $18,500 major work in 24 months + ~ $42,000 in drivetrain/hydraulic over 18 months.

• Proposed action: Motion to authorize purchase of one (1) 2026 Freightliner 114SD tandem‑axle with Henderson stainless body, front/wing/underbody, pre‑wet/de‑icing from Nuss Truck & Equipment via State of MN cooperative contract, $298,750 incl. tax/delivery. Budgeted in Equipment Replacement Fund 402‑43100‑5800; 2026 CIP shows $300,000.

• Avoid: any mention of Mom’s February 2023 blizzard hip fracture on Oakridge, Ruth’s Stage III cancer diagnosis and oncology schedule, Mark Jensen’s alcohol treatment relapse at Essentia, Tom Erickson’s grievance about his October 2024 verbal warning for the joke about Hannah’s anxiety, or my January 14, 2024 stress ER visit.

• After mtg: quick debrief with Karen O. about Equipment Replacement Fund balance and how the $12,600 restitution stream from Dan’s January 2024 DWI/property damage case is being coded (internal only).

metadata.title: Council Mtg – Plow Truck Authorization (Unit #23 Replacement)

artifacts.content:
Thread with Emily – storms, kids, and my schedule

[2025-02-11 15:47]
Michael: Hey, today’s shaping up to be another mess. Radar looks ugly over the Brainerd High/Forestview corridor.

[15:49]
Emily: You still going to make your 4:30 Northern Pines appointment?

[15:52]
Michael: That’s the plan, but if Unit 23 pulls another stunt like it did by BHS on Feb 11 last year and dies right before I head out, I’m stuck. Missing counseling after Dad’s March 2023 stuff already screwed with my head more than I like to admit.

[15:55]
Emily: I know. Just don’t push yourself into another Essentia ER night like Jan 14, 2024. Remember what Dr. Nguyen said.

[16:01]
Michael: I remember. Also still thinking about Hannah’s slide‑off on CR 3 by Harrison on Jan 2 and that near‑miss at Oakridge & Riverside last winter when the 2009 plow was in at the Thiesse Road garage for brakes. Deputy basically told me we needed better cutting action. I keep picturing her standing in those ruts.

[16:05]
Emily: She’s nervous about driving tonight too. I can swing by the rink and grab Jake if you’re tied up.

[16:08]
Michael: Please do. If the storm ramps up, I’ll be out till at least midnight. Last time Unit 23 died by St. Joe’s in December I had to call Mark Jensen in for 6 hours of OT and Karen Schultz is still side‑eyeing me about the $1,150 overage after that October HR talk.

[16:11]
Emily: Okay. I’ll cover kids. Just text if you end up skipping Northern Pines so we can reschedule.

[16:13]
Michael: Deal. One more winter of this and that Freightliner can’t come soon enough.

Subject: Random life update – hockey, budgets, and too many hospitals

Hey [Name],

Figured I’d send a proper update instead of just liking your fishing pics.

Work first: I’m still herding cats at Brainerd Public Works – Streets Division. Biggest headache is that cursed Unit #23, our 2006 International plow. It’s failed during 11 snow operations over three winters. In December 2024 its hydraulics quit by St. Joseph’s Medical Center and I had to call in part‑timer Mark Jensen for a 6‑hour OT shift, which pushed our overtime line $1,150 over and earned me a “chat” with City Engineer Karen Schultz and HR in October about keeping OT under control. Meanwhile I’m trying to get council to bless a 2026 Freightliner 114SD from Nuss for $298,750 under the state contract.

Home: Emily’s still at the high school, juggling students’ crises and our own. Hannah’s a junior now – between hockey, AP classes, and that January 2, 2025 slide‑off on County Road 3 by Harrison in her Civic, she’s had enough winter drama. Jacob’s in 7th, hockey‑obsessed, and still rehabbing the ACL he blew in March 2023. Cuyuna Regional did a good job, but we’re on the hook for about $18,700 out of pocket, so every time I sign another repair order for Unit 23 I think “this is like paying for another surgery on something that’s already shot.”

Mom’s hanging in there. Still in PT from the February 2023 hip fracture on unplowed Oakridge and now doing oncology at St. Joe’s–Essentia after a Stage III breast cancer diagnosis in November 2024. I’m burning most of my vacation/comp time hauling her to treatments while trying not to miss my own counseling at Northern Pines after that January 14, 2024 Essentia ER scare.

If we ever get a quiet weekend and clear ice, I’d love to meet you on Gull for a half‑day. I could use a morning where the only thing I’m watching is a bobber, not a radar loop.

Take care,
Mike

metadata.subject: Random life update – hockey, budgets, and too many hospitals
